condition.qualitative_description: Block-level SOGE exceeds 70% or CGWB classifies the block as Semi-critical, Critical, or Over-exploited, indicating extraction pressure on the aquifer.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: CGWB defines groundwater stress classes based on extraction as a percentage of net annual recharge. In hard rock volcanic and crystalline basement aquifers of the Deccan Plateau (AER-6, AER-3), storativity is inherently low and recharge is spatially heterogeneous. Even a block-level 'Safe' classification above 60% can mask critical depletion at the micro-watershed scale. Over-exploited blocks in Marathwada and Telangana are well documented in CGWB national compilations.

condition.qualitative_description: The linear trend in annual groundwater recharge (P - ET - Runoff) is negative and the multi-year mean is also negative, indicating chronic depletion rather than a single drought year.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: A persistently negative delta_g trend means outflows (ET + runoff) chronically exceed precipitation, leaving no surplus for aquifer recharge. In semi-arid hard rock settings of the Deccan Plateau, even small annual deficits accumulate rapidly due to low aquifer storage. Global analyses confirm that rapid groundwater-level declines (>0.5 m/year) are widespread in dry regions with extensive croplands, and that acceleration of decline is detectable through multi-year trend analysis.

condition.qualitative_description: Dominant aquifer class is hard rock (volcanic basalt or crystalline basement) with less than 20% alluvial lithology, confirming low storativity and limited recharge capacity.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Hard rock aquifers of the Deccan Plateau — volcanic basalt (AER-6: Vidarbha, Marathwada, Telangana) and crystalline basement (AER-3: Karnataka, western Andhra Pradesh) — have very low storativity and depend on fractures and weathered zones for storage. Over-exploitation in these systems manifests as seasonal emptying and filling rather than gradual table decline seen in alluvial aquifers. Low alluvial fraction confirms that natural recharge buffering is minimal, making the system highly vulnerable to extraction pressure.

condition.qualitative_description: Fewer than 5 cumulative MGNREGA soil and water conservation works recorded in the micro-watershed, indicating underinvestment in recharge infrastructure.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Low historical SWC investment means that natural recharge opportunities — check dams, percolation ponds, farm bunds, trench structures — have not been exploited. India has a long tradition of tank and pond-based managed aquifer recharge; more than 500,000 tanks and ponds have historically been used to detain monsoon runoff and replenish aquifers. Low MGNREGA SWC counts signal an opportunity for intervention rather than a terminal condition, and directly inform the recommended solutions (trench and check-dam construction, community pond desilting).

condition.qualitative_description: Farmers report that borewell or dug-well depth has increased over recent years, or that shallow dug wells have gone dry during rabi or zaid seasons, indicating a declining static water level in the hard rock aquifer.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Deepening well depth is a direct proxy for declining groundwater table. In hard rock aquifers where storage is limited to weathered and fractured zones typically 10–30 m deep, even modest annual declines can cause shallow dug wells to fail. This variable is not available in the framework data and must be elicited from farmers or field surveys. Farmer-reported well deepening or seasonal well failure is a strong qualitative confirmation of the groundwater stress pathway.

condition.qualitative_description: Farmers report moderate-to-high borewell/tubewell density (roughly 10–50 or more than 50 within 2–3 km) in hard rock terrain, indicating localised over-extraction not captured by block-level SOGE. Very low density or widespread borewell failure may indicate aquifer limits rather than density-driven stress.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: High local borewell density confirms extraction pressure at micro-watershed scale when paired with landscape depletion signals. Failed or unsuccessful borewells indicate hard-rock aquifer limits and may amplify stress severity without confirming high density per se.

condition.qualitative_description: Farmers report salty/brackish well water, white crust on soil or pipes, or water unsuitable for sensitive crops — indicating salinity from deep penetration or over-extraction.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Reported salinity confirms groundwater stress as a quality-and-quantity problem; may also indicate a distinct salinity pathway requiring solution reassessment.

overall_reasoning_note: Groundwater stress in hard rock aquifers of the Deccan Plateau should be confirmed by at least two of the three primary signals: elevated SOGE (sig_01), chronic negative delta_g trend (sig_02), or farmer-reported well deepening (sig_05). Hard rock aquifer class (sig_03) is a structural amplifier — it does not confirm stress on its own but raises the severity of any extraction signal. Low MGNREGA SWC investment (sig_04) amplifies depletion risk and identifies intervention opportunities. If SOGE is Safe but delta_g trend is persistently negative, prioritise the water balance signal — block-level SOGE masks micro-watershed heterogeneity in fractured hard rock terrain. Always ask the user about borewell density and recent well failure before finalising diagnosis.

confounders.confounder: Meteorological drought causing apparent groundwater stress through reduced recharge rather than over-extraction
confounders.how_to_distinguish: Compare drought_moderate_return_period and mean_annual_precipitation_mm against historical baseline. If drought years co-occur with the negative delta_g trend, drought is the primary driver and groundwater stress is secondary. Check soge_dev_percent — if it is low (<50%) but delta_g is still negative, drought rather than extraction is likely the primary cause. In semi-arid Deccan settings, rainfall variability is high and a single severe drought can mimic chronic depletion signals.

confounders.confounder: Expansion of irrigated double-cropping increasing ET demand and reducing apparent recharge, rather than aquifer structural limitation
confounders.how_to_distinguish: Check trend_double_crop_area_ha and trend_cropping_intensity. If double-cropped area has expanded significantly in recent years alongside the negative delta_g trend, increased consumptive demand (not aquifer depletion per se) may be the proximate driver. This distinction matters for solutions: demand-side management (crop diversification, micro-irrigation) is more appropriate than recharge infrastructure alone.

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Has the depth of your borewell or dug-well increased over the past 5–10 years? If yes, by roughly how many feet or metres? Have any shallow dug wells in your village gone completely dry during the rabi or summer season?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: A reported increase in well depth of more than 3 metres over 8 years (~0.4 m/year) strongly confirms the groundwater stress pathway in hard rock aquifers. Seasonal dug-well failure during rabi or zaid is a near-definitive confirmation of the seasonal emptying characteristic of low-storativity volcanic and crystalline basement aquifers.

choices.label: Well depth has stayed roughly the same (within ~1 m)
Wells have deepened by more than 2–3 m in the last 5–10 years
Springs or shallow wells have dried up permanently or for much longer

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Roughly how many borewells or tubewells are there in your village or the surrounding area? Would you say there are very few (fewer than 10), a moderate number (10–50), or a large number (more than 50 within 2–3 km)?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: High borewell density in a hard rock aquifer with moderate SOGE confirms localised over-extraction not captured by the block-level SOGE figure, strongly confirming the groundwater stress pathway. It also informs the urgency of collective groundwater governance interventions.

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Have farmers noticed that borewell or well water has become saltier, leaves white deposits on soil or pipes, or is no longer suitable for certain sensitive crops?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: Salinity in hard rock aquifers can indicate deep borewell penetration into saline zones — a secondary consequence of over-extraction — or a distinct causal pathway (saline intrusion or geogenic salinity) that changes solution recommendations away from recharge infrastructure toward water quality treatment or source substitution.

choices.label: No — water quality has not changed; no saltiness or deposits
Some salinity or white deposits; still usable for some crops
Brackish or salty water; crop damage or unsuitable for irrigation

sources.title: National Compilation of Dynamic Ground Water Resources of India 2022
sources.relevance_note: Defines SOGE class thresholds (Safe <70%, Semi-critical 70–90%, Critical 90–100%, Over-exploited >100%) used in sig_01; documents over-exploited blocks in Marathwada, Vidarbha, and Telangana.

sources.title: Rapid groundwater decline and some cases of recovery in aquifers globally
sources.relevance_note: Global synthesis of 170,000 monitoring wells showing rapid groundwater-level declines (>0.5 m/year) are widespread in dry regions with extensive croplands; confirms trend-based detection approach used in sig_02.

sources.title: Addressing Depletion in Alluvial Aquifers
sources.relevance_note: Distinguishes hard rock peninsular aquifer dynamics (seasonal emptying and filling, low storativity) from alluvial aquifer depletion; supports sig_03 rationale and the need for geology-specific management approaches.

sources.title: Sixty years of global progress in managed aquifer recharge
sources.relevance_note: Documents India's long history of tank and percolation pond-based recharge (>500,000 structures); supports sig_04 rationale and recommended solutions including check-dam and trench construction for in-situ recharge in semi-arid hard rock settings.

sources.title: Groundwater use for irrigation – a global inventory
sources.relevance_note: Establishes India as the world's largest user of groundwater for irrigation (39 million ha); contextualises the scale of extraction pressure driving groundwater stress in agricultural regions including the Deccan Plateau.
